titleBlock.headline: Senolytics improve physical function and increase lifespan in old age
titleBlock.authors: Xu, M., Pirtskhalava, T., Farr, J.N., Kirkland, J.L. et al.

researchSummary:
This is a summary of the scientific study.
Not affiliated with the study authors or their institutions.
whatAreSenolytics: As we age, some cells suffer DNA damage but refuse to die. They enter a state called 'senescence'. They sit in your tissues, secreting toxic chemicals (SASP) that cause inflammation and accelerate aging.
Senolytics are a new class of drugs designed specifically to hunt down and force these zombie cells to undergo apoptosis (programmed cell death) without harming healthy cells.
Researchers discovered that combining Dasatinib (a leukemia drug) and Quercetin (an apple/onion flavonoid) creates a potent senolytic cocktail that dramatically extends healthspan and lifespan.

studyOverview.label: Objective
studyOverview.value: Determine if clearing senescent cells with small molecules extends lifespan.

studyOverview.label: The Intervention
studyOverview.value: Intermittent dosing of Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) in aged mice.

studyOverview.label: Physical Function
studyOverview.value: Treated mice showed massive improvements in grip strength and treadmill endurance.

mechanismDescription: Senescent cells secrete a toxic cocktail of inflammatory cytokines called the Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype (SASP). This poisons neighboring cells, turning them into zombies too. The D+Q cocktail disables the 'anti-death' pathways that these zombie cells rely on, causing them to self-destruct and halting the spread of inflammation.

hitAndRunProtocol.title: The Hit-and-Run Protocol
hitAndRunProtocol.description: Unlike traditional drugs (like blood pressure medication) which must be taken every day, Senolytics use a 'Hit-and-Run' approach. Because senescent cells take weeks or months to accumulate, you only need to take the drug intermittently (e.g., 3 days a month).

hit.title: The Hit (Days 1-3)
hit.desc: High dose of D+Q clears out the accumulated senescent cells.

run.title: The Run (Days 4-30)
run.desc: No drugs are taken. Inflammation drops, tissues heal, and side effects are avoided.

keyFindings.title: Reversing Frailty
keyFindings.desc: When D+Q was given to naturally aged, 20-month-old mice (equivalent to 65-year-old humans), it rapidly improved walking speed, hanging endurance, and grip strength.

keyFindings.title: The 36% Extension
keyFindings.desc: Treatment initiated in very old age extended the remaining lifespan of the mice by 36% and delayed the onset of age-related fatal diseases.

keyFindings.title: Stopping the Spread
keyFindings.desc: The study proved that injecting just a small number of senescent cells into young mice was enough to make them frail. Clearing them with D+Q halted this infectious spread.

keyFindings.title: Intermittent Efficacy
keyFindings.desc: Continuous dosing was not required. Because the drugs clear the cells, the benefits persist long after the drug has left the bloodstream.

conclusions: Senescent cells are a primary driver of physical frailty and decreased survival in old age.
The Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) combination is a highly potent, orally bioavailable senolytic therapy.
Intermittent 'hit-and-run' dosing is sufficient to clear cells, extend lifespan, and minimize off-target toxicity.
protocolRecommendations: Dasatinib is a prescription chemotherapy drug and carries significant side effects. It is not currently recommended for off-label longevity use without strict physician oversight.
However, this study launched dozens of human clinical trials testing D+Q for conditions like Alzheimer's, osteoarthritis, and chronic kidney disease.
Fisetin (a flavonoid found in strawberries) is currently being studied as a natural, over-the-counter alternative to Dasatinib with senolytic properties.
